Title: The Shikimate Pathway and Aromatic Amino Acid Biosynthesis in Plants

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Upstream signaling component involved in defense-associated transcriptional activation
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: precursor for auxin and defense-related indole metabolites
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: plastids; exported to cytosol for downstream metabolism
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts upstream in defense-related induction of the Trp pathway; ups1 mutant shows reduced PAT expression, implying reduced activation of Trp biosynthesis under stress
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UPS1 was identified as an upstream component required for full pathogen- and stress-inducible expression of PAT. 2. The ups1 mutant shows compromised salicylate-, jasmonate-, and reactive oxygen species-mediated induction of defense gene expression. 3. UPS1 therefore contributes to transcriptional activation of the Trp pathway during defense responses.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; expression analysis of PAT and defense genes under salicylate/jasmonate/ROS-related conditions
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Implied by mutant phenotype under pathogen and stress responses
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: pathogen and stress; salicylate; jasmonate; reactive oxygen species
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for coordinating defense-induced Trp pathway activation, but too upstream and pleiotropic for straightforward metabolic engineering
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely broad defense and signaling pleiotropy
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a core summarized regulatory component of AAA pathway activation, but exact molecular identity/function remains unresolved in this review.

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: F-box protein; component of E3 ubiquitin ligase complex in jasmonate perception
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: precursor for auxin and defense-related indole metabolites
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: plastids; exported to cytosol for downstream metabolism
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for methyl jasmonate-induced expression of DAHP synthase and Trp pathway genes
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Methyl jasmonate induces genes encoding DAHP synthase and Trp pathway enzymes in Arabidopsis. 2. This induction occurs via a COI1-dependent signaling pathway. 3. COI1 links jasmonate signaling to increased aromatic amino acid pathway gene expression.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Hormone treatment and mutant/signaling dependency analysis
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Responsive to methyl jasmonate signaling context
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: May be useful for inducible defense-associated activation of AAA metabolism, but broad signaling role likely causes pleiotropy
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad jasmonate pathway effects on growth and defense balance
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review highlights COI1-dependent induction of AAA pathway genes, although COI1 is not a pathway enzyme or transcription factor.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Type II DAHP synthase; metalloenzyme with core (β/α)8 barrel and AAA-binding additions
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Plants (e.g., Arabidopsis thaliana, Solanum tuberosum, Solanum lycopersicum)
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Solanum tuberosum; Solanum lycopersicum
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Solanum tuberosum; Solanum lycopersicum
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Entry step of aromatic amino acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes aldol condensation of phosphoenolpyruvate and D-erythrose 4-phosphate to form DAHP
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protein biosynthesis and precursors for lignin, phenylpropanoids, alkaloids, auxin, glucosinolates, tocopherols and other specialized metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: plastids; products exported for cytosolic downstream metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls carbon entry into the shikimate pathway; inducible DAHPS1 supports increased flux under wounding/pathogen conditions
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Plant DAHPS1 is one of two DAHP synthase isoforms and is strongly induced by wounding and pathogen infection. 2. Arabidopsis DAHP synthase 1 is redox regulated by the ferredoxin-thioredoxin system, linking pathway flux to photosynthetic electron flow. 3. DAHP synthase catalyzes the first committed step supplying carbon to all aromatic amino acids.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene isolation by complementation; expression analysis; biochemical enzyme characterization; redox regulation assays
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Induced by wounding and pathogen infection
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: wounding; pathogen infection; photosynthetic redox state
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential lever to increase upstream carbon flux into AAA metabolism, especially under inducible contexts; regulatory complexity remains unresolved
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altering early pathway flux may broadly perturb multiple downstream specialized metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Plant DAHP synthases are generally not feedback inhibited by AAAs, unlike microbial enzymes; in vitro activation by Trp and inhibition by arogenate reported in some species.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Type II DAHP synthase; metalloenzyme
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Plants (e.g., Arabidopsis thaliana, Solanum tuberosum, Solanum lycopersicum)
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Solanum tuberosum; Solanum lycopersicum
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Solanum tuberosum; Solanum lycopersicum
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Entry step of aromatic amino acid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes aldol condensation of phosphoenolpyruvate and D-erythrose 4-phosphate to form DAHP
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protein biosynthesis and precursors for many specialized metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Constitutively expressed DAHP synthase isoform supporting basal AAA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Plant DAHPS2 is a constitutively expressed isoform. 2. It provides basal carbon flux into the shikimate pathway. 3. Differential expression of DAHPS1 and DAHPS2 indicates functional specialization of DAHP synthase isoforms.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene isolation by complementation; expression analysis; biochemical characterization
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for increasing baseline AAA flux, but role as broad upstream gateway may cause pleiotropic effects
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad metabolic perturbation possible
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A putative additional cytosolic Co2+-dependent DAHP synthase activity exists in plants, but the gene is unknown.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Monofunctional enzyme requiring NAD+ and divalent cation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts DAHP to 3-dehydroquinate through a multistep intramolecular rearrangement sequence in one active site
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: proteinogenic amino acids and precursors of many defense and nutritional metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports shikimate pathway flux; root- and elicitor-associated expression suggests role in stress-responsive AAA metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A plant DHQS gene was isolated from tomato by complementation of an E. coli mutant. 2. The gene is highly expressed in roots and induced by elicitor treatment in suspension cells. 3. Plant DHQS is a monofunctional plastid-localized enzyme in the shikimate pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant complementation; expression analysis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in roots; induced by elicitor treatment
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in roots; elicitor inducible
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential upstream flux target, but direct engineering value not established
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: NA
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Fungal homologs occur in the pentafunctional AROM complex, unlike plant monofunctional DHQS.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Bifunctional fused DHD-SDH enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis thaliana; Nicotiana tabacum and other plants
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Sequentially dehydrates 3-dehydroquinate to 3-dehydroshikimate and reduces 3-dehydroshikimate to shikimate using NADPH
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: precursors for lignin, chlorogenic acid, auxin, and other aromatic metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: RNAi suppression of plastidic DHD-SDH in tobacco lowers Phe, Tyr, lignin, and chlorogenic acid and causes accumulation of pathway intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. In plants, DHD and SDH are fused into a bifunctional enzyme with adjacent active sites. 2. Tobacco RNAi suppression of plastidic DHD-SDH causes accumulation of 3-dehydroquinate and shikimate and reduces Phe, Tyr, lignin, and chlorogenic acid. 3. The enzyme is therefore required for efficient shikimate pathway flux into aromatic amino acid-derived metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Crystal structure; RNAi suppression; metabolite profiling; subcellular localization
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Single gene in most plants; tobacco has plastidic and cytosolic isoforms
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important for sustaining aromatic amino acid and phenylpropanoid flux, but suppression causes strong metabolic penalties
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced lignin and chlorogenic acid; broad metabolic disruption
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mechanism for elevated shikimate in RNAi lines remains unclear; role of cytosolic tobacco DHD-SDH2 unresolved.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: ATP-dependent kinase with core, lid, shikimate-binding and nucleotide-binding domains
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa; Solanum lycopersicum; Spinacia oleracea
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa; Solanum lycopersicum; Spinacia oleracea
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Oryza sativa; Solanum lycopersicum; Spinacia oleracea
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Phosphorylates shikimate at the C3 hydroxyl using ATP
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: proteinogenic amino acids and precursors of multiple specialized metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely links shikimate pathway flux to cellular energy balance
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Plants possess species-dependent numbers of SK isozymes. 2. Purified spinach SK is modulated by cellular energy charge. 3. SK may connect energy status to carbon flux through the shikimate pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical enzyme assays; gene isolation/localization
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Possible target for tuning flux under energy-limited states, but direct engineering evidence is lacking
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May couple strongly to broader energy metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Species differ in isozyme copy number.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Class I plant EPSP synthase; glyphosate-sensitive
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis thaliana; Solanum lycopersicum; Petunia hybrida and other plants
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Solanum lycopersicum; Petunia hybrida
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Solanum lycopersicum; Petunia hybrida
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers the enolpyruvyl moiety of PEP to shikimate 3-phosphate to form EPSP
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: major herbicide target; not otherwise established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: proteinogenic amino acids and specialized metabolite precursors
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for chorismate formation; glyphosate inhibition reduces AAA biosynthesis and induces compensatory pathway responses
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. EPSP synthase catalyzes the penultimate step of the shikimate pathway. 2. Plant EPSP synthases are glyphosate-sensitive class I enzymes. 3. In petunia, expression is generally low but tissue- and developmentally regulated in flowers, consistent with roles in Phe-derived volatile formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene isolation; enzyme structural studies; herbicide inhibition evidence; expression analysis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Constitutive low expression with tissue/developmental regulation in petunia flowers
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Flower tissue- and developmentally regulated in petunia
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Widely used target for herbicide resistance engineering via glyphosate-resistant microbial EPSPS; native expression also relevant to floral volatile traits
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altering EPSPS may broadly affect AAA-derived metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Arabidopsis contains one functional and one putative EPSP synthase gene.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Monofunctional FMN-dependent enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes anti-elimination of phosphate and hydrogen from EPSP to form chorismate using reduced FMN
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: terminal determinant for chorismate formation; redox-linked
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: universal precursor node for aromatic amino acid and downstream metabolite biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides chorismate, the universal precursor for all AAA branches; activity depends on reduced FMN/redox state
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CS catalyzes the final step of the shikimate pathway to produce chorismate. 2. Although the reaction is redox neutral, plant CS requires reduced FMN from an external source. 3. Tomato contains two differentially expressed CS genes, indicating regulatory diversification.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene isolation; biochemical/structural characterization; expression analysis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Two differentially expressed genes in tomato
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: external flavin reductase activity (not physically associated)
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential choke point for boosting chorismate supply, but cofactor/redox constraints complicate engineering
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad effects across all chorismate-derived pathways
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Source of reduced FMN in plastids remains unresolved.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Large catalytic subunit of anthranilate synthase; feedback-sensitive Trp-binding regulatory site
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis thaliana; Ruta graveolens; Nicotiana tabacum; Oryza sativa and other plants
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Ruta graveolens; Nicotiana tabacum; Oryza sativa
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Nicotiana tabacum; Oryza sativa; Astragalus sinicus
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: committed step; branch point from chorismate
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes conversion of chorismate to anthranilate with pyruvate elimination using ammonia provided by ASβ
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directs branch selection toward target product
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: major committed and feedback-regulated control step
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protein biosynthesis; precursor for auxin, indole glucosinolates, phytoalexins, and terpenoid indole alkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Feedback-insensitive ASα overexpression strongly increases free Trp accumulation, up to hundreds-fold depending on system
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ASα catalyzes the committed step of Trp biosynthesis and mediates Trp feedback inhibition. 2. Plants usually possess multiple ASα genes, with one constitutive and another defense-inducible isoform. 3. Overexpression of feedback-insensitive ASα in tobacco, rice, potato, Arabidopsis, and Astragalus strongly increases Trp, demonstrating ASα as a key control point.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical feedback assays; mutant analysis; transgenic overexpression of feedback-insensitive alleles; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: One constitutive isoform and one developmentally/stress-induced isoform; induced by wounding and pathogens
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Constitutive plus defense-inducible expression depending on isoform
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: wounding; pathogen infection; methyl jasmonate; salicylate-related defense context
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value engineering target for Trp biofortification and boosting Trp-derived metabolites; feedback-insensitive alleles are especially effective
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can alter auxin and other indole metabolites; may redistribute chorismate flux
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Tobacco and Ruta graveolens ASα include feedback-insensitive natural variants.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Glutamine amidase small subunit of anthranilate synthase
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes glutamine and transfers ammonia to ASα within anthranilate synthase complex
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: required for direct formation of target branch intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: precursor to auxin and diverse indole metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential catalytic partner enabling anthranilate synthase activity in Trp biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ASβ provides ammonia to ASα by glutamine hydrolysis. 2. Plant AS functions as a noncovalent enzyme complex. 3. ASβ is required for anthranilate formation but feedback inhibition is primarily mediated by ASα.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical enzyme-complex characterization; gene identification
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Supportive target in Trp pathway engineering, but ASα is the preferred control point
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: NA
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Feedback inhibition by Trp is attributed mainly to ASα rather than ASβ.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Monofunctional transferase
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers phosphoribosyl moiety from phosphoribosylpyrophosphate to anthranilate
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms pathway intermediate toward target product
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protein synthesis and precursor of auxin/indole compounds
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutation in PAT causes Trp biosynthetic defect, anthranilate glucoside accumulation, and auxin-deficient phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis trp1 mutant carries a lesion in the single-copy PAT gene. 2. PAT deficiency causes anthranilate glucoside accumulation and blue fluorescence. 3. trp1 also shows auxin-deficient phenotypes not rescued by Trp feeding, supporting Trp-independent auxin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant mapping/identification; phenotype analysis; feeding experiments; expression analysis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Constitutive; first two introns enhance mRNA abundance posttranscriptionally
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Posttranscriptional enhancement of mRNA abundance by first two introns
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful genetic marker for Trp pathway function, but not highlighted as a major engineering lever
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Auxin-deficient growth phenotypes when impaired
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PAT expression is used as a readout in defense-related regulation studies.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Isomerase family; multiple highly similar paralogs in Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes irreversible rearrangement of phosphoribosylanthranilate to CdRP
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms pathway intermediate toward target product
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protein synthesis and precursor of indole metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduction of PAI activity causes blue fluorescence phenotype associated with Trp pathway disruption
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis contains three or four highly similar PAI genes depending on ecotype. 2. PAI1 and PAI2 encode functional enzymes and are differentially stress- and cell-type-regulated. 3. Reduced PAI activity in antisense plants or trp6 mutant causes blue fluorescence, confirming pathway function.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Antisense suppression; mutant analysis; expression analysis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Differentially expressed in response to environmental stresses and cell type-specific contexts
Pathway_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Coordinated epigenetic control of the PAI gene family in some Arabidopsis ecotypes
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Limited direct engineering value due to gene family redundancy and epigenetic complexity
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Redundancy complicates targeted manipulation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Relative contribution of each paralog remains unresolved.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Monofunctional synthase
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Trp core pathway and auxin branch-point context
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Irreversibly converts CdRP to indole-3-glycerol phosphate
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protein synthesis and precursor of auxin and indole specialized metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Antisense suppression reduces both Trp and auxin, indicating indole-3-glycerol phosphate is a key branch-point intermediate in Trp-independent auxin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IGPS1 was isolated by complementation of an E. coli trpC mutant. 2. Antisense suppression lowers both Trp and auxin. 3. The data support indole-3-glycerol phosphate as a key branch-point intermediate linking Trp metabolism and Trp-independent auxin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Complementation; antisense suppression; metabolite analysis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential branch-point engineering node, though likely accompanied by hormone-related pleiotropy
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Changes in auxin levels
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Arabidopsis contains two IGPS genes, but only IGPS1 is specifically discussed functionally.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: TSα catalytic subunit; forms α2β2 complex with TSβ
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes retro-aldol cleavage of indole-3-glycerol phosphate to indole and glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protein synthesis; precursor of auxin and indole metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Arabidopsis trp3 mutant establishes TSα1 as indispensable for Trp biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TSα catalyzes the first of the final two reactions in Trp biosynthesis. 2. TSα and TSβ form an α2β2 heterocomplex that channels indole. 3. Arabidopsis TSα1 is genetically proven essential by the trp3 auxotrophic mutant.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; enzyme-complex characterization
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Fundamental target for pathway completion, though not emphasized as a major flux-control engineering target
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss causes Trp auxotrophy
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: TSα paralogs also evolved specialized indole-producing enzymes such as IGL and BX1.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: PLP-dependent TSβ catalytic subunit
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses indole and serine to produce tryptophan using PLP
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protein synthesis and precursor of indole metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Arabidopsis trp2 mutant proves TSβ1 is indispensable for Trp biosynthesis; maize has redundant TSβ genes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TSβ catalyzes the final reaction producing Trp from indole and serine. 2. Arabidopsis TSβ1 is essential, as shown by the trp2 auxotrophic mutant. 3. In maize, two redundant TSβ genes must both be lost to generate Trp auxotrophy.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; enzyme-complex characterization
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for understanding pathway redundancy and maintaining basal Trp production
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss causes Trp auxotrophy
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Indole channeling by TS complex prevents diversion to other indole-derived products.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: TSα paralog specialized for indole formation independent of TSβ
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts indole-3-glycerol phosphate to indole independent of a TSβ-like subunit
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: natural pesticides / defense compounds
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: plastids and downstream extra-plastidic sites not detailed
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes the first step in benzoxazinoid biosynthesis from the Trp pathway intermediate indole-3-glycerol phosphate
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BX1 is a TSα paralog that produces indole independently of TSβ. 2. It catalyzes the first dedicated step of benzoxazinoid biosynthesis in maize. 3. BX1 exemplifies diversion of Trp-pathway intermediates into specialized defense metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization; comparative functional analysis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High relevance for engineering benzoxazinoid-based defense chemistry
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May compete with Trp production for indole-3-glycerol phosphate
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not a direct AAA biosynthesis enzyme for proteinogenic Trp, but a core summarized branch enzyme affecting indole metabolite allocation.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: TSα paralog specialized for volatile indole formation
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Indole volatile formation from Trp-pathway intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Produces volatile indole from indole-3-glycerol phosphate independently of TSβ
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: branch enzyme for volatile indole production
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: herbivore-induced defense signaling
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Generates volatile indole in maize under herbivore attack
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IGL is a TSα paralog that converts indole-3-glycerol phosphate to indole independently of TSβ. 2. In maize, IGL is responsible for volatile indole formation during herbivore attack. 3. This branch competes with Trp formation for the same precursor.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical and functional characterization in defense context
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for enhancing inducible volatile defense
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Could divert precursor away from Trp biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review emphasizes branch competition from indole-3-glycerol phosphate.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: AroQ-type chorismate mutase
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis thaliana; Petunia hybrida and other plants
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Committed step from chorismate to prephenate
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts chorismate to prephenate via Claisen rearrangement
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directs branch selection toward Phe/Tyr biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protein synthesis; precursors of phenylpropanoids, benzenoids, lignin, tocopherols and other aromatic metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: RNAi suppression of petunia CM1 reduces formation of Phe-derived volatile phenylpropanoid/benzenoid compounds in flower petals
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CM1 catalyzes the first committed step toward Phe/Tyr biosynthesis in plastids. 2. CM1 is inhibited by Phe/Tyr and activated by Trp, linking branch competition among AAAs. 3. Petunia CM1 suppression reduces Phe-derived floral volatiles, confirming in planta contribution to specialized metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNAi suppression; biochemical allosteric characterization; expression/localization studies
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Inducible by elicitors and pathogen treatments in Arabidopsis/petunia
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Inducible by elicitors and pathogens; active in flower petals
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for increasing flux into Phe-derived aroma and phenylpropanoid pathways; allosteric regulation must be considered
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter Trp/Phe/Tyr partitioning
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CM inhibition constants for Phe/Tyr are relatively high compared with ADT/ADH feedback sensitivity.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: AroQ-type chorismate mutase lacking plastid transit peptide
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Arabidopsis; Petunia; Tobacco context for cytosolic chorismate pool
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Petunia hybrida; Nicotiana tabacum
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts chorismate to prephenate in the cytosol
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directs branch selection toward Phe/Tyr biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: precursors of cytosolic phenylpropanoid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: cytosolic downstream metabolism after plastid export
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides high-affinity cytosolic chorismate conversion capacity; physiological role remains unresolved
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CM2 lacks a plastid transit peptide and localizes outside plastids. 2. It is generally insensitive to allosteric regulation by AAAs and has much higher affinity for chorismate than plastidic CM1. 3. CM2 likely functions where cytosolic chorismate concentration is low, but its exact physiological role remains unclear.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene isolation; biochemical characterization; GFP localization/protein import assays
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: caffeic acid inhibition in tobacco context
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential route to manipulate cytosolic aromatic flux, but physiological uncertainty remains
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Unknown due to unresolved biological role
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Tobacco CM2 is inhibited by caffeic acid, a cytosolic phenylpropanoid intermediate.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: AroQ-type chorismate mutase
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts chorismate to prephenate
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directs branch selection toward Phe/Tyr biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: precursor supply for phenylpropanoids and Tyr-derived metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Additional plastidic allosterically regulated CM isoform with intermediate kinetic properties
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis CM3 contains a plastid transit peptide and is allosterically regulated like CM1. 2. Its affinity for chorismate is closer to that of cytosolic CM2. 3. CM3 displays differential expression and may serve distinct physiological roles.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization; expression analysis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Differential expression; inducible to lesser extent than CM1
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential fine-tuning target for plastidic Phe/Tyr flux
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: NA
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Role relative to CM1 and CM2 remains unresolved.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Class Ib aspartate aminotransferase with Lys109 important for dicarboxylic acid recognition
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis thaliana; Petunia hybrida; Solanum lycopersicum
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Petunia hybrida; Solanum lycopersicum
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Petunia hybrida; Solanum lycopersicum
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reversibly transaminates prephenate to arogenate using glutamate or aspartate as amino donor
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: not rate-limiting relative to ADT in petunia petals
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: precursors for phenylpropanoids, benzenoids, lignin, vitamin E branch inputs
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Predominantly directs flux from prephenate to arogenate; RNAi suppression in petunia reduces total PPA-AT but not total AspAT activity, confirming specific in planta role
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PPA-AT genes from Arabidopsis, petunia, and tomato were recently identified and shown to encode class Ib aminotransferases. 2. Plant PPA-ATs have much higher affinity for prephenate than arogenate, favoring forward flux to arogenate. 3. Petunia RNAi confirms major role in prephenate-to-arogenate conversion, while ADT rather than PPA-AT appears rate-limiting for Phe formation in petals.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene identification; kinetic enzyme assays; RNAi suppression; activity assays
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for reinforcing arogenate-route flux, but ADT may be a more effective engineering target for elevating Phe
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad aminotransferase family may complicate specificity interpretations
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Recombinant enzymes also display AspAT activity, but in planta contribution to total AspAT activity is minor.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Catalytic dehydratase domain plus C-terminal ACT regulatory domain
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis thaliana; Petunia hybrida; Oryza sativa
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Petunia hybrida; Oryza sativa
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Petunia hybrida; Oryza sativa
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: terminal step of arogenate Phe route
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts arogenate to phenylalanine by dehydration and decarboxylation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: rate-limiting/key control step in petunia arogenate pathway; major feedback control by Phe
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protein synthesis; major precursor for lignin, phenylpropanoids, benzenoids and many defense compounds
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: RNAi suppression reduces Phe levels by ~80% in petunia flowers; feedback-insensitive ADT mutants accumulate high Phe (and also high Trp) in Arabidopsis and rice
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Plant ADTs are plastidic monofunctional enzymes with strict or preferred specificity for arogenate over prephenate. 2. RNAi suppression of ADT in petunia flowers reduces Phe by about 80%, demonstrating that the arogenate route predominates. 3. Comparative activity analysis indicates ADT, rather than PPA-AT, is rate-limiting within the petunia arogenate pathway. 4. Mutations in the ACT regulatory domain that reduce Phe sensitivity cause elevated Phe accumulation in Arabidopsis and rice.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant enzyme assays; RNAi suppression; mutant analysis; metabolite profiling; localization studies
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Active in flowers; etiolated seedlings for detectable PDT activity in Arabidopsis context
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: exogenous shikimate feeding reveals contribution of phenylpyruvate route; developmental tissue context
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Prime target for increasing Phe and Phe-derived metabolites using feedback-insensitive alleles or expression tuning
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May perturb flux balance among Phe, Tyr, and Trp and alter specialized metabolism broadly
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Some plant ADTs can also use prephenate weakly, allowing conditional phenylpyruvate-route contribution.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Tetrameric dehydrogenase with N-terminal nucleotide-binding and C-terminal substrate-binding domains
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: terminal step of arogenate Tyr route
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively decarboxylates arogenate to tyrosine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protein synthesis and precursor to tocopherols/tocotrienols via 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate/homogentisate
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Arabidopsis ADH1 is a strict arogenate dehydrogenase; maize adh1 disruption causes opaque endosperm and reduced zein with only minor Tyr decrease
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Plant ADHs are plastid-targeted and primarily use arogenate to make Tyr. 2. Arabidopsis ADH1 is highly specific for arogenate, whereas ADH2 can weakly accept prephenate. 3. A maize ADH1 transposon insertion causes opaque endosperm and reduced zein storage proteins, indicating importance for kernel metabolism though free Tyr decreases only slightly.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene isolation; biochemical characterization; localization studies; mutant analysis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to redirect flux to Tyr-derived metabolites such as vitamin E precursors, though redundancy may reduce effect
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Kernel quality defects observed in maize mutant
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In vivo functions of Arabidopsis ADHs were not fully investigated in this review.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Broad-specificity aromatic amino acid aminotransferase
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Cucumis melo; Papaver somniferum; Arabidopsis thaliana
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Cucumis melo; Papaver somniferum; Arabidopsis thaliana
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Cucumis melo; Papaver somniferum; Arabidopsis thaliana
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: terminal step of alternative routes; also can function in catabolism reverse direction
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reversibly transaminates phenylpyruvate to Phe and 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate to Tyr using PLP
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms target product in alternative routes
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protein synthesis and precursors of numerous aromatic metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Likely cytosolic for melon enzyme lacking plastid transit peptide
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Biochemically capable of Phe/Tyr formation, but in planta role in primary Phe/Tyr biosynthesis remains unproven
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Plant AAA aminotransferases have broad substrate specificity similar to microbial enzymes. 2. Genes were identified from melon, opium poppy, and Arabidopsis, and melon enzyme likely localizes in cytosol. 3. Despite biochemical activity, their in planta roles in Phe/Tyr biosynthesis remain unproven; opium poppy enzyme instead has demonstrated function in alkaloid biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization; gene isolation; virus-induced gene silencing for alkaloid pathway role in opium poppy
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential but unvalidated target for alternative Phe/Tyr route engineering
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad substrate specificity may cause off-target metabolic effects
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review emphasizes unresolved molecular identity of aminotransferases truly responsible for alternative Phe/Tyr biosynthesis in planta.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Phe-specific aromatic amino acid hydroxylase
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Pinus taeda; Physcomitrella patens; Chlamydomonas reinhardtii
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Pinus taeda; Physcomitrella patens; Chlamydomonas reinhardtii
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Pinus taeda; Physcomitrella patens; Chlamydomonas reinhardtii
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates phenylalanine to tyrosine using 10-formyltetrahydrofolate as cofactor
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: converts one terminal AAA into another
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: protein synthesis and precursor of Tyr-derived metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides a plant-specific folate-linked route from Phe to Tyr in nonflowering plants
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Phe hydroxylases were identified in gymnosperms, mosses, and algae but not angiosperms. 2. These enzymes localize to plastids. 3. They establish a novel link between folate metabolism and aromatic amino acid metabolism in nonflowering plants.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene identification and biochemical characterization
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Interesting route for engineering Tyr supply in selected lineages, but absent from angiosperms
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Lineage-specific applicability
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not present in flowering plants according to this review.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: indole glucosinolates; upstream shikimate-derived aromatic amino acid flux
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Coordinates upstream AAA pathway gene expression with indole glucosinolate biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes precursor supply from the shikimate pathway toward indole glucosinolate metabolism
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HIG1/MYB51 regulates indole glucosinolate biosynthesis in Arabidopsis. 2. It also activates the upstream shikimate pathway gene DAHP synthase 1. 3. This shows coordinated regulation of specialized metabolism and its aromatic amino acid precursor supply.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transcription factor functional studies summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Defense/developmental cues not fully detailed here
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Likely feedforward coordination of precursor pathway with downstream specialized metabolism
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for coordinated engineering of indole glucosinolate production with precursor pathway activation
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct binding was not specified in the text excerpt.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Trp branch biosynthesis and downstream indole specialized metabolism
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Coordinates Trp precursor biosynthesis with indole glucosinolate pathway demand
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enhances transcription of ASα1, promoting committed-step entry into Trp biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ATR1/MYB34 regulates indole glucosinolate biosynthesis in Arabidopsis. 2. It also activates expression of ASα1 in the Trp pathway. 3. This illustrates shared transcriptional control of specialized metabolism and upstream Trp biosynthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transcription factor functional studies summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for jointly increasing Trp-pathway flux and indole glucosinolates
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review does not specify whether activation is direct or indirect here.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: precursor supply for terpenoid indole alkaloid biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Trp biosynthesis and downstream alkaloid biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Controls ASα1 expression and several terpenoid indole alkaloid biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: ASα1; several terpenoid indole alkaloid biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Links upstream Trp production to downstream alkaloid biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes ASα1 expression to support precursor supply for terpenoid indole alkaloid formation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ORCA3 regulates several genes of terpenoid indole alkaloid biosynthesis in Catharanthus roseus. 2. ASα1 is also under ORCA3 control. 3. ORCA3 therefore coordinates Trp precursor supply with alkaloid pathway demand.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transcription factor functional studies summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Associated with terpenoid indole alkaloid-producing tissues/conditions
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising regulator for stacked engineering of Trp supply and alkaloid biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely broad reprogramming of specialized metabolism
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Directness of ASα1 activation was not resolved in this review.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: shikimate pathway precursor supply for specialized metabolism
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Suppression reduces expression of all seven shikimate pathway genes
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major regulator connecting upstream aromatic precursor synthesis to phenylpropanoid-polyamine conjugate production
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: RNAi suppression eliminates phenylpropanoid-polyamine conjugates by reducing expression of all seven shikimate pathway genes
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB8 suppression in Nicotiana attenuata strongly reduces all seven shikimate pathway genes. 2. This results in complete elimination of phenylpropanoid-polyamine conjugates. 3. MYB8 is therefore a powerful coordinator of aromatic precursor supply for downstream defense metabolites.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNAi suppression; expression analysis; metabolite profiling
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Feedforward activation of upstream precursor pathway
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for coordinated enhancement of phenylpropanoid-derived defense metabolites
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad rerouting of primary and specialized aromatic metabolism
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct binding to all seven promoters was not demonstrated in the review text.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: phenylalanine-derived wall phenolics / secondary wall components
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Defect with NST3 reduces expression of genes encoding 6 of 10 enzymes required for Phe biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Six of ten Phe biosynthetic enzyme genes (not individually listed in excerpt)
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Coordinates Phe biosynthesis with secondary wall formation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required with NST3 for normal expression of multiple Phe biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NST1 and NST3 are NAC transcription factors regulating secondary wall formation. 2. An Arabidopsis double mutant defective in NST1 and NST3 shows reduced expression of genes encoding 6 of 10 enzymes required for Phe biosynthesis. 3. These factors therefore connect wall formation programs with aromatic amino acid precursor supply.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Likely shared developmental regulatory network with NST3
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Feedforward coupling to downstream wall biosynthesis demand
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Could coordinate lignified wall traits with precursor biosynthesis, but likely substantial developmental pleiotropy
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct targets were not resolved here.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: phenylalanine-derived wall phenolics / secondary wall components
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Defect with NST1 reduces expression of genes encoding 6 of 10 enzymes required for Phe biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Six of ten Phe biosynthetic enzyme genes (not individually listed in excerpt)
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Coordinates Phe biosynthesis with secondary wall formation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required with NST1 for normal expression of multiple Phe biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NST3 works with NST1 in secondary wall formation. 2. Loss of both reduces expression of many Phe biosynthetic genes. 3. This supports coordinated regulation between wall biogenesis and aromatic precursor synthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Likely shared developmental regulatory network with NST1
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Feedforward coupling to downstream wall biosynthesis demand
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential tool for coordinated wall/aromatic pathway engineering, with likely severe pleiotropy
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct promoter binding not established here.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: shikimate pathway precursor supply for floral volatile biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Directly binds and activates EPSP synthase promoter
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Controls spatial and developmental expression of a key shikimate-pathway gene supporting floral volatile production
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes EPSP synthase expression in petunia flowers to support aromatic precursor supply
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. EPF1 is a petunia C2H2 zinc finger regulator of shikimate pathway genes. 2. It directly binds the EPSP synthase promoter. 3. EPF1 controls spatial and developmental expression of EPSP synthase in flowers, helping drive Phe-derived volatile production.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Promoter binding assay; expression analysis in flowers
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: developmental and diurnal floral regulation context
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for floral scent engineering through upstream precursor pathway activation
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter broader aromatic metabolism in petals
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: One of three petunia TFs highlighted as controlling shikimate pathway genes.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: shikimate pathway and Phe-derived volatile biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Suppression reduces CM and PAL expression; also affects DAHP synthase and EPSP synthase expression
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: CM; PAL; DAHP synthase; EPSP synthase
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major regulator coupling upstream shikimate flux and downstream phenylpropanoid volatile formation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes expression of shikimate and phenylpropanoid pathway genes to support floral scent production
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ODORANT1 is a petunia R2R3-MYB regulating Phe-derived floral volatiles. 2. RNAi suppression reduces expression of CM and PAL and also lowers DAHP synthase and EPSP synthase. 3. ODORANT1 therefore coordinates both precursor supply and downstream scent biosynthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Developmental and diurnal floral cues; EOBII partially upstream
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Feedforward coordination of precursor and product pathways
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target for engineering floral aroma via coordinated multigene activation
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter multiple aromatic branches in flowers
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Directness of all target regulations was not fully established.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Phe-derived volatile biosynthesis and partial upstream precursor regulation
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Suppression reduces CM and PAL; binds ODORANT1 promoter and partially downregulates ODORANT1 when suppressed
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Acts within a petunia floral scent regulatory hierarchy controlling both upstream and downstream genes
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports expression of ODORANT1 and volatile-related genes, thereby promoting floral scent metabolism
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. EOBII is a petunia R2R3-MYB with partial target overlap with ODORANT1. 2. EOBII suppression reduces CM and PAL expression but not DAHP synthase or EPSP synthase. 3. EOBII binds the ODORANT1 promoter and its suppression partially downregulates ODORANT1, indicating hierarchical control.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNAi suppression; promoter binding assay; overexpression analysis
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Additional factor(s) likely involved in EOBII-mediated activation
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for hierarchical floral scent engineering, especially with ODORANT1 network components
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Overexpression of EOBII alone did not significantly alter ODORANT1 and CM expression, implying missing cofactors.
